steps.title: Find the gesture (pencil)
steps.description: Lightly sketch a long S-curve from where the feet will stand up through where the head will end. Herons hold a beautiful tall S — neck folded, body forward, legs straight. This single line carries the whole pose.

steps.title: Block the body and head (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, sketch a slender oval for the body about a third of the way up your S, and a small oval for the head at the top. The neck connects them as a graceful curve along your gesture line.

steps.title: Block the legs (pencil)
steps.description: From the bottom of the body, sketch two long thin lines straight down to the water. Add a small bend at the knee on each — herons stand with a slight angle, not ramrod straight. Mark short horizontal ticks where the feet rest.

steps.title: Block the bill (pencil)
steps.description: From the front of the head, sketch a long sharp wedge — the bill is roughly as long as the head itself, tapering to a point. Compare the bill length to the head before committing.

steps.title: Switch to ink and trace the silhouette
steps.description: Switch to ink. Trace the head, neck, and body in one mostly continuous flowing pass — let the S-curve guide your stroke. Use a confident hand; herons read as elegance, and tentative lines kill that.

steps.title: Ink the bill and eye (ink)
steps.description: Still in ink, trace the bill as two long tapering strokes meeting at the point. Add a tiny dot for the eye and a short angled line for the line of the mouth running back from the bill.

steps.title: Ink the legs (ink)
steps.description: Trace each leg as a single long line from body to foot — moving from your shoulder keeps the line straight. At each foot, add three short forward-facing toes and one short rear toe. Stop the back leg's line where the front leg crosses it.

steps.title: Switch back to pencil for the water (pencil)
steps.description: Switch back to pencil. Add two or three short horizontal marks at the waterline where the legs enter the water — soft ripples that anchor the bird in its environment. Pencil weight keeps them quiet.

steps.title: Switch to ink and suggest the feathers (ink)
steps.description: Switch to ink one more time. Add a few short hatching marks along the back and a couple of longer drooping strokes near the tail for the plume feathers. Resist drawing every feather — a handful suggests the rest.
